stem: Two cyclists start from the same point on a trail. Cyclist A departs and travels at 16 miles per hour. Cyclist B starts 1 hour after Cyclist A and travels at 24 miles per hour in the same direction. How many miles from the starting point will Cyclist B catch up to Cyclist A?
explanation: In 1 hour, Cyclist A travels 16 × 1 = 16 miles before Cyclist B starts. Let t be the time in hours after Cyclist B starts. When they meet, both have traveled the same distance from the starting point: distance of A = 16 + 16t, distance of B = 24t. Setting equal: 16 + 16t = 24t, so 16 = 8t, giving t = 2 hours. Distance from start = 24 × 2 = 48 miles. Verification: Cyclist A traveled 16 × 3 = 48 miles in 3 hours, and Cyclist B traveled 24 × 2 = 48 miles in 2 hours.

distractorRationale.A: Used the head-start time as the catch-up time: 24 × 1 = 24.
distractorRationale.B: Found the correct catch-up time of 2 hours but multiplied by Cyclist A's speed instead of Cyclist B's speed: 16 × 2 = 32.
distractorRationale.C: Correct: A has a 16-mile head start. Setting 16 + 16t = 24t gives t = 2 hours. Distance = 24 × 2 = 48.
distractorRationale.D: Multiplied Cyclist B's speed by Cyclist A's total elapsed time of 3 hours instead of Cyclist B's travel time of 2 hours: 24 × 3 = 72.
